content: Has anyone looked at using rust's async for anything?
I expect the answer is a solid no, but the async wg was curious if RfL had any use cases. Specifically relating to https://github.com/rust-lang/libs-team/issues/191
We did have async usage during “rust” time: we had a workqueue backed async runtime. I believe there’s some usage in networking and filesystem back then.
I also made my timer binding provide a Delay future
Actually, the async runtime and async ecosystem are in my next year plan :)
Yeah, see e.g. https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/tree/rust/rust/kernel/kasync and https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust/samples/rust/rust_echo_server.rs
@Wedson Almeida Filho talked about it in OSS NA last year and recently in Netdevconf 0x17
Now you mentioned it, I think one of the issue that klint found is related to async ;-)
In kangrejos 2022 I talked about a 9p server implemented with async rust as well: https://kangrejos.com/Async%20Rust%20and%209p%20server.pdf
I was looking to resurrect the async runting and post some Patch but this is not a good time for me

Reading @Asahi Lina 's description of the drm scheduler and fences, it kinda sounded like something that could be made very ergonomic if it could be abstracted away via futures/async :thinking: That being said I know next to nothing about the kernel really so here I am perusing zulip for whether or not this idea has been discussed, heh.

Just now looking at this. Had an incredible development experience using the embassy async runtime for complex bare-metal embedded systems. I am sure it would definitely find uses in the kernel and is actually a unique feature that only rust currently brings to the table, although mixing the async code with the current blocking utilities may be complicated / have footguns. I see that a minimal async runtime was planned initially, what's the current status on it?

Brian Misiak said:

Reading Asahi Lina 's description of the drm scheduler and fences, it kinda sounded like something that could be made very ergonomic if it could be abstracted away via futures/async :thinking: That being said I know next to nothing about the kernel really so here I am perusing zulip for whether or not this idea has been discussed, heh.

I don't think it would be that useful because you already have a job concept and the point of the scheduler is to do the work scheduling around fences, which kind of duplicates an async runtime. In general fences aren't used for kernel work, they are used for hardware work, and the chaining is userspace-driven, so I don't see how an async programming style would help much. I guess you could do it for job dependency/resource acquisition? But I don't even do that in my driver right now (resources are acquired synchronously at submit time).

There's also the thing where fence signaling cannot do any allocations, so if the async runtime does any allocations behind the scenes it's not suitable in that context either.

Personally, I was thinking mostly general usage as state-machine simplifier / converting callbacks to linear code. Nightly features such as type-alias-impl-trait can be used to statically calculate task size and do no allocations. I don't have experience with kernel development, just now dipping my toes into it so take my thoughts with a grain of salt.

The thing is there isn't really a variable state machine. The lifecycle of the DRM job is mostly fixed and driven by the scheduler, you just provide callbacks to do certain things. 90% of the code is just blocking and runs on the submission ioctl, then the other callbacks just check for prerequisites, submit the job to the hardware, and when it completes you signal the fence. You could write it out as an async function, sure, but it wouldn't make it any easier to understand, and it would allow for misuse because then you could choose not to perform operations in the right expected order.

I think the only part that could be usefully written as async code is the prepare_job callback that is supposed to acquire hardware resources and return a new fence for each one. You'd need a DRM fence-driven async runtime for that. But as I said... my driver doesn't even do that in that callback, it just checks for space in the hw job queue and if not returns the oldest available fence, so there isn't any open-coded state machine there either, just a single piece of code that does one thing.

I acquire resources in the blocking path ahead of time instead, and that works fine because the GPU has ~64 slots for every exhaustible resource so it's very unlikely to block in real-world scenarios because nobody ever has 64 threads/queues competing for GPU resources all at once (and if it does happen it'll still make forward progress fine, there's no fairness guarantee but an async runtime wouldn't necessarily guarantee that either on its own. I actually have a mode where I serialize all GPU execution by limiting one resource to 1 slot and it runs fine and barely has a performance impact for regular usage).
https://github.com/AsahiLinux/linux/blob/asahi-wip/drivers/gpu/drm/asahi/queue/mod.rs#L221 no real state machine there.

Oh, I am not the one talking about drm, this was Brian. I don't have nearly the minimal qualifications to assume anything about any kernel subsystem, just commented because I have experience with using async rust code on baremetal systems and it has been a great experience compared to older blocking-based rtos approaches using blocking code. Main advantage is converting callback-based state machines to linear code especially due to being able to take advantage of code flow constructs for the state machines.

If you mean async in general, Wedson worked on async Rust in the kernel and presented it at least a couple times. He had a couple executors at least (one workqueue-based, another single-threaded). Some bits are in the old rust branch, e.g. rust/kernel/kasync.rs and samples/rust/rust_echo_server.rs; others probably in his repository.

I was discussing this with @Daniel Almeida the other day, and while I think you could use async with fences, I'm not sure it gives you that much.

Asahi Lina said:

There's also the thing where fence signaling cannot do any allocations, so if the async runtime does any allocations behind the scenes it's not suitable in that context either.

Not even Tokio allocates in the codepaths that matter here. Being allocation-free is definitely doable.
